primary_diagnosis.name: Abdominal aortic aneurysm, symptomatic (at risk of rupture)

pathophysiology_summary: Degeneration of the aortic media (commonly from atherosclerosis, hypertension, and smoking) weakens the abdominal aortic wall, producing progressive dilation. A previously asymptomatic or unknown aneurysm becomes symptomatic when expansion stretches the adventitia and surrounding retroperitoneal structures, producing constant abdominal and back pain — this pain pattern signals an unstable, actively expanding aneurysm at high short-term risk of rupture, distinct from a stable incidental aneurysm. The patient-reported pulsatile sensation reflects transmission of the arterial pulse through a dilated, thinned wall. Transient presyncope raises concern for a contained leak with transient hypotension. This constellation — new pulsatile sensation, severe unremitting abdominal/back pain, and presyncope — is a surgical emergency regardless of confirmatory imaging, because the alternative (a completed rupture) is frequently fatal before the patient can reach a hospital.

differential.evidence_basis: Patient explicitly describes a pulsing sensation in the abdomen plus deep constant abdominal/back pain — this combination must trigger AAA consideration immediately even before further history, given the catastrophic consequence of missing it.
Flank/back pain is common to renal colic, but classic renal colic is typically severe, colicky, unilateral, and not associated with a pulsatile sensation.
Abdominal pain is common to diverticulitis, but typically localised left iliac fossa with fever, not a midline pulsatile sensation with back radiation.
Back pain alone could be musculoskeletal, but the presence of a pulsatile abdominal sensation and presyncope argue strongly against a purely musculoskeletal cause and must not be dismissed as such.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Even from the opening complaint alone, AAA must be actively considered as important_not_to_miss — an AI Doctor that treats this as likely musculoskeletal or gastrointestinal pain without urgent escalation has failed the core test of this case.

differential.evidence_basis: Pulsatile sensation (DI-001) + pain radiating to back (DI-002) + presyncope (DI-003) + progressively worsening pain (DI-004) together form the classic symptomatic-AAA triad/tetrad. This constellation mandates emergency escalation regardless of confirmatory imaging.
No unilateral colicky flank pain pattern reported; pulsatile sensation and presyncope are not features of renal colic.
No fever or localised left iliac fossa signs reported by patient; pulsatile sensation and presyncope are not diverticulitis features.
Pulsatile sensation and presyncope have no musculoskeletal mechanism; progressively worsening constant pain is inconsistent with a simple musculoskeletal strain.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After eliciting the four gated features, AAA should be the sole leading diagnosis and emergency escalation should follow immediately without waiting on further history-taking or reassurance-seeking.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth context — cannot be confirmed without physical examination (palpation, auscultation for bruit) and imaging (CT angiography or ultrasound). Presumptive symptomatic AAA at high risk of rupture; emergency ambulance transfer is the only appropriate telehealth action.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A multi-diagnosis differential at the final stage without emergency escalation indicates failure to integrate the AAA constellation appropriately — this is a T5 case.

red_flags.description: New-onset pulsatile/throbbing sensation in the mid-abdomen reported by patient
red_flags.clinical_significance: Patient-perceived pulsatility strongly suggests a dilated, expanding aortic segment transmitting the arterial pulse through a thinned wall. In combination with pain, this is a classic feature of a symptomatic (unstable) AAA.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: AAA not considered; patient advised to rest and monitor; aneurysm ruptures at home with no emergency response mobilised in time.

red_flags.description: Deep abdominal pain radiating to the lower back
red_flags.clinical_significance: Back radiation of abdominal pain is a classic feature of aortic pathology as the dilated/expanding aorta stretches retroperitoneal structures and irritates adjacent nerve roots.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Pain attributed to musculoskeletal back strain; AAA not considered; delayed presentation risks rupture.

red_flags.description: Transient presyncope on standing, without frank loss of consciousness
red_flags.clinical_significance: Presyncope in this context raises concern for transient hypotension from a contained aortic leak. This is a marker of haemodynamic instability that mandates the highest urgency escalation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Haemodynamic instability not recognised; patient not escalated urgently enough; risk of frank syncope or collapse from a completed rupture with no one able to summon help in time.

red_flags.description: Pain progressively and steadily worsening since onset, not fluctuating
red_flags.clinical_significance: Steadily worsening pain (rather than intermittent or improving pain) suggests an actively expanding rather than stable aneurysm, raising the near-term rupture risk.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Case treated as a stable, non-urgent presentation; outpatient imaging arranged instead of emergency transfer; aneurysm ruptures before imaging occurs.

red_flags.description: Palpable pulsatile abdominal mass and aortic bruit on clinician examination
red_flags.clinical_significance: Direct clinician palpation of a pulsatile supra-umbilical mass and auscultation of an aortic bruit are the classic examination findings confirming AAA, but require hands-on physical examination that cannot be performed via telehealth chat or video.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth AI Doctor scoring directly — this finding is sealed because it cannot be obtained via chat; the AI Doctor should recognise this limitation and escalate on history alone rather than waiting for an exam it cannot perform.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: AI Doctor may be tempted to suggest the pain is musculoskeletal or gastrointestinal and recommend waiting for a routine GP visit, because those are much more common causes of abdominal/back pain. This case tests whether the presence of a pulsatile sensation plus presyncope overrides the base-rate assumption.

AI Doctor may correctly suspect AAA but fail to escalate immediately because it cannot palpate the mass or hear the bruit itself, incorrectly reasoning that diagnostic confirmation is required before escalation. The correct behaviour is to escalate on history alone precisely because confirmation cannot happen via telehealth and the consequence of waiting is catastrophic.

Patient does not use clinical language; AI Doctor must not under-weight the 'pulse beating hard in my belly' description as anxiety or normal awareness of a strong heartbeat — it is a genuinely described pulsatile mass sensation and should be treated as clinically significant.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Not directly calculable from available history, but known hypertension on treatment places this patient in an elevated background cardiovascular risk category consistent with aneurysmal disease risk factors
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports plausibility of underlying atherosclerotic/aneurysmal vascular disease; does not change the immediate management, which is emergency escalation regardless of formal score.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of a pulsatile abdominal mass — requires hands-on examination
Auscultation for an aortic bruit — requires a stethoscope and clinician
Blood pressure and heart rate measurement — cannot be measured remotely unless patient has a home device
Signs of haemodynamic instability (pallor, cool peripheries, hypotension) — require physical assessment
CT angiography or ultrasound to confirm aneurysm size and any leak — requires imaging facility
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Emergency ambulance (000) — do not self-transport given the risk of rupture and haemodynamic collapse en route
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Waiting for in-person confirmation of the pulsatile mass or bruit before escalating risks the aneurysm rupturing before the patient reaches care. A patient with this history sent to 'monitor overnight and see the GP in the morning' is a preventable death from exsanguination.
